q: Gottman's research shows enduring couples keep detailed love maps — a constantly updated knowledge of each other's inner world.
who: Ask one curious question about each other's day
why: You can never finish learning a person. Ask anyway, daily.

q: Partners who keep learning about each other resist the 'I already know you' trap.
who: Note something new in each other's inner world
why: Curiosity is what keeps a long love from going stale.

q: Gottman found masters turn toward bids for attention about 86% of the time, versus 33% for couples who later separated.
who: Respond to small bids instead of half-ignoring
why: The tiny moments — a sigh, a 'look at this' — are where love is actually won or lost.

q: The first three minutes of a disagreement strongly predict how it ends.
why: How a conversation starts is usually how it ends. Start softly.

q: Repair attempts are the single biggest difference between stable and unstable couples.
why: It's not avoiding fights that saves couples — it's reaching for each other mid-fight.

q: Closing on common ground prevents the unresolved residue that quietly accumulates into resentment over months.
who: End hard talks with one point of agreement
why: Agree on one small thing before you stop. Resentment grows in what's left open.

q: Reflecting proves you actually received your partner — the core of perceived responsiveness.
who: Reflect back what your partner said before replying
why: Repeat back what you heard. Being understood matters more than being agreed with.

q: A small concrete action outperforms reassurance because it shows you were listening and you followed through.
who: Do one small thing your partner mentioned needing
why: Don't just say it will be okay — do one small thing that proves you listened.

q: Undivided attention is the cheapest and rarest gift you can give in a distracted age.
who: Put the phone down when your partner is talking
why: Full attention, even briefly, says: you matter more than everything on this screen.

q: Small kept promises build a felt sense of safety far more than occasional grand gestures.
who: Do what you said you'd do
why: Trust isn't built in speeches. It's built in small promises quietly kept.

q: A partner who can be reached when needed becomes a secure base.
why: Answer when it counts. Being reachable is what makes someone feel safe.

q: Quick repair teaches the relationship a vital lesson: bad moments are survivable.
who: Name and fix any small rupture same-day
why: Fix the small scratch today so it never becomes a scar.

q: Rituals create reliable points of contact that survive busy weeks.
why: One tiny ritual — the morning coffee, the goodnight text — holds the week together.

q: Couples with a shared sense of direction report more meaning and more resilience under stress.
why: Talk about where you two are going. A shared direction carries you through stress.

q: Studies link natural 'we'-talk to higher satisfaction and better conflict recovery.
why: Say 'we' more than 'you and I'. Language quietly shapes the team.

q: The Arons' self-expansion research shows novel shared activity reignites the excitement that routine quietly erodes.
why: Try one new thing together. Novelty brings back the early-days spark.

q: Bringing new ideas into the relationship keeps each of you interesting to the other — a steady defence against the slide into boredom.
why: Stay interesting to each other — share what's growing in your own mind.

q: Deliberate novelty resists hedonic adaptation — the slow numbing to good things we already have, including a good partner.
why: Break the routine on purpose before the routine numbs you to each other.

q: Specific gratitude — naming the actual act — makes a partner feel truly seen, and predicts higher commitment far more than a vague 'thanks'.
why: Thank them for the specific thing. Vague gratitude bounces off; precise gratitude lands.

q: Naming effort that usually goes unseen directly counters the drift toward taking each other for granted — a top quiet killer of relationships.
why: Point out the invisible work. Feeling unseen is how love quietly leaks away.

q: Expressed admiration builds fondness — the buffer Gottman found protects couples through their hardest seasons.
who: Tell your partner one thing you admire
why: Say what you admire out loud. Fondness spoken becomes armour for hard seasons.

q: Stress spillover is well documented: tension carried in from outside gets discharged onto the nearest person.
why: Leave the day at the door. Your stress isn't your partner's fault.

q: Buffering couple time from outside pressure preserves the relationship's repair capacity — the reserve you draw on when things get hard.
why: Guard a small pocket of time that belongs only to the two of you.

q: Sharing a hard day builds intimacy; making a partner absorb it erodes it.
who: Don't dump work stress on each other
why: Share the hard day — don't hand it over to carry.

q: A hand on the shoulder is a small daily biological deposit of safety.
why: A touch in passing says 'I'm here' without needing a single word.

q: Sincere compliments sustain positive sentiment — the lens through which neutral or clumsy acts later get read kindly instead of harshly.
why: Compliments build the kind eyes through which everything else gets read.

q: A roughly 20-second hug or 6-second kiss crosses the threshold where bonding chemistry actually engages.
why: Hold the hug a little longer. The body needs time to believe it.

q: People who see relationships as cultivated persist through rough patches; 'soulmate' believers quit sooner when reality gets difficult.
who: Treat friction as buildable, not fatal
why: Love isn't found, it's grown. Growers outlast believers in magic.

q: Relationships are maintained by accumulated small choices, not sustained by feeling alone.
why: Feelings fluctuate; choices accumulate. Choose one small effort today.

q: A charitable read of ambiguous behaviour breaks the negative-interpretation spiral in which everything a partner does gets read as a slight.
why: When in doubt, assume they meant well. It's usually true, and it changes everything.

q: No coldness, no silence as punishment. We ask directly and kindly.
why: Warmth even in disagreement. Silence used as a weapon wounds twice.

q: No unresolved hurt goes to sleep with us.
why: Never let a hurt spend the night unspoken.

q: Small and early, spoken or written. No suffering alone overnight.
why: Say it while it's still small. Waiting turns a pebble into a wall.

q: Fully accepted, history included. It never enters a disagreement.
why: What's been accepted stays accepted. Old chapters don't fight new battles.

q: Neither pressure nor retreat.
why: Move at the speed of the slower heart, together.

q: Phones away, fully present.
why: One evening a week where nothing and no one else exists.

q: Unhurried, private, listening to each other's bodies.
why: Intimacy grows from safety, never from hurry.

q: We show up loudly for good news, not just faithfully for bad.
why: Being loudly happy for each other counts as much as being there in trouble.

q: Gratitude unspoken doesn't count.
why: Gratitude only works out loud.

q: Review, adjust, keep building.
why: Every few months, look at the relationship itself — tend the garden, not just the flowers.

q: Kate Muir's How to Have a Magnificent Midlife Crisis, distilled. Eighteen chapters with questions to read together.
why: The middle years, taken as an invitation instead of a crisis.

q: The science of desire that lasts — Perel, Nagoski, Aron, Mark & Lasslo. Seven practices to keep the flame alive.
why: Desire that lasts is a practice, not an accident.

q: An interactive dice-and-card game for two. Six tiers, 216 unique card-experiences. For one evening with no rush.
why: Play is how grown-ups stay young together.
